originalTitle: What is happening to UK prices?
summary: The war in Iran is expected to push UK Inflation further above the Bank of England's 2% target.
content:
Inflation in the UK was 2.8% in the year to May, according to new figures from the Office for National Statistics.

That's the same rate as the previous month, but is still above the Bank of England's target of 2%.

Economists had expected a slight rise to 3%, as the effects of the war in the Middle East filtered down to consumers.

The recent peace agreement between the US and Iran means that inflation could peak at a lower point than experts had forecast, but it is still expected to climb further.

Inflation is the increase in the price of something over time.

For example, if a bottle of milk costs £1 but is £1.05 a year later, then annual milk inflation is 5%.

How is the UK's inflation rate measured?

The prices of hundreds of everyday items, including food and fuel, are tracked by the Office for National Statistics (ONS).

This virtual "basket of goods" is regularly updated to reflect shopping trends, with alcohol-free beer, dashboard cameras, and pet grooming equipment among items added in 2026 , while premium bottled lager and sheets of wrapping paper were removed.

The ONS uses price changes in the basket of goods over the previous 12 months to calculate inflation.

The main inflation measure is called the Consumer Prices Index (CPI) , and the latest figure is published every month.

Although the May CPI figure of 2.8% remains above the Bank of England's 2% target, it is well below the 11.1% figure reached in October 2022.

That was the highest rate for 40 years.

The May reading – which meant prices were still going up but the rate of increase had not changed – surprised economists.

Most analysts thought inflation would rise to 3%, mainly driven by the war in the Middle East.

But a sharp fall in food inflation counteracted increases elsewhere.

It comes after a fall in April, which was largely due to the reduction in the government's energy price cap for the three months between April to June. That cut annual bills by £117 for a household using a typical amount of energy.

However the next cap, which takes effect on 1 July, is expected to be higher, reflecting increased wholesale energy costs.

Precisely because food or energy prices can be very volatile, the Bank of England also considers other measures, such as "core inflation", when deciding whether and how to change rates.

Core CPI was 2.6% in the 12 months to May, up from 2.5% in April.

UK inflation had been expected to be at or around the target level of 2% over the next five years, according to the official forecasts published alongside Chancellor Rachel Reeves' Spring Statement in March.

But those predictions were made before the Iran war.

In April, the Bank of England warned that UK inflation could go as high as 6% in the worst-case scenario.

The peace deal means that inflation may not go as high as expected. Oil prices fell sharply after the deal was announced.

Analysts have warned, however, that if the deal collapses oil prices may rebound, which could cause inflation to rise.

How the Iran war may affect your money and bills

How high could UK petrol and diesel prices go?

What is happening to gas and electricity prices?

What other factors push inflation up?

Although inflation has fallen significantly since the October 2022 high, prices have not fallen. They have just been rising less quickly.

Inflation soared in 2022 because oil and gas were in greater demand after the Covid pandemic, and energy prices surged again when Russia invaded Ukraine.

It then remained well above the 2% target, partly because of higher food prices.

The figures show food price inflation fell from 3% in the year to April to 2.2% in the year to May.

The rate of inflation for bread, meat, pasta and vegetables all dropped compared to last year.

The Agriculture and Horticulture Development Board said that British households are struggling to afford beef at the moment, meaning demand for the product has fallen.

At the same time, while the number of cows being slaughtered has fallen, carcasses weigh more so there's been oversupply.

The ONS says that the price of beef and veal (it lumps them both together) rose by 9.3% in May which, admittedly is still high.

But compared to previous months when prices jumped by as much as 27%, the slowdown is evident.

However, it can take seven to 13 months for cost increases in the food supply chain to feed through to prices on supermarket shelves.

The Food and Drink Federation, which represents manufacturers, said that current food prices don't reflect the impact of the Strait of Hormuz closing.

It warned that food inflation was likely to rise this year and into 2027.

Employees who face higher living costs are more likely to push for pay rises.

Firms who are already facing higher staff costs as a result of increased employer National Insurance contributions and minimum wage hikes are then under pressure to put up their own prices. This can in turn contribute to higher inflation.

Why does putting up interest rates help to lower inflation?

The Bank of England is to decide on Thursday whether to raise, lower or hold its base interest rate.

When inflation was well above its 2% target, the Bank of England increased interest rates to 5.25%, a 16-year high.

The idea is to make borrowing more expensive, giving people and businesses less money to spend. This can encourage people to save more.

In turn, this reduces demand for goods and slows price rises.

But it is a balancing act – increasing borrowing costs risks harming the economy.

For example, homeowners face higher mortgage repayments, which can outweigh better savings deals.

Businesses also borrow less, making them less likely to create jobs. Some may cut staff and reduce investment.

In recent months, inflation has remained above the Bank's target. At the same time, the economy has remained relatively flat and the jobs market has softened.

Therefore, the Bank has chosen to cut rates, despite high inflation, in an attempt to encourage p
chineseTitle: 英国的物价发生了什么变化？
chineseSummary: 伊朗战争预计将推动英国通胀进一步高于英国央行 2% 的目标。
chineseContent:
根据英国国家统计局的最新数据，截至 5 月份的一年里，英国的通货膨胀率为 2.8%。

该利率与上个月相同，但仍高于英国央行 2% 的目标。

由于中东战争的影响波及到消费者身上，经济学家原本预计该数字将小幅上涨至 3%。

美国和伊朗最近达成的和平协议意味着通胀峰值可能低于专家预测，但预计仍将进一步攀升。

通货膨胀是某种东西的价格随着时间的推移而上涨。

例如，如果一瓶牛奶的价格为 1 英镑，但一年后价格为 1.05 英镑，则每年的牛奶通胀率为 5%。

英国的通货膨胀率是如何衡量的？

国家统计局 (ONS) 跟踪包括食品和燃料在内的数百种日常用品的价格。

这个虚拟“商品篮”会定期更新，以反映购物趋势，2026 年添加的商品包括无酒精啤酒、行车记录仪和宠物美容设备，而优质瓶装啤酒和包装纸则被删除。

ONS 使用过去 12 个月内一篮子商品的价格变化来计算通货膨胀。

主要的通胀指标称为消费者价格指数（CPI），最新数据每月发布。

尽管 5 月份 2.8% 的 CPI 数字仍高于英国央行 2% 的目标，但远低于 2022 年 10 月达到的 11.1% 的数字。

这是 40 年来的最高比率。

5 月份的数据——这意味着价格仍在上涨，但增长率没有改变——令经济学家感到惊讶。

大多数分析师认为通胀将升至3%，主要是受中东战争推动。

但食品通胀的大幅下降抵消了其他地方的通胀上涨。

此前，四月份出现了下降，这主要是由于政府降低了四月至六月三个月的能源价格上限。对于一个使用典型能源量的家庭来说，这每年可以减少 117 英镑的费用。

然而，下一个上限将于 7 月 1 日生效，预计会更高，反映出批发能源成本的增加。

正是由于食品或能源价格可能非常波动，英国央行在决定是否以及如何改变利率时还会考虑其他指标，例如“核心通胀”。

截至 5 月份的 12 个月内，核心 CPI 为 2.6%，高于 4 月份的 2.5%。

根据英国财政大臣雷切尔·里夫斯 (Rachel Reeves) 3 月份春季声明同时发布的官方预测，英国通胀预计未来五年将达到或接近 2% 的目标水平。

但这些预测是在伊朗战争之前做出的。

4月份，英国央行警告称，在最坏的情况下，英国通胀率可能高达6%。

和平协议意味着通货膨胀可能不会像预期的那么高。交易宣布后，油价大幅下跌。

不过，分析师警告称，如果协议破裂，油价可能会反弹，从而导致通胀上升。

伊朗战争可能如何影响您的金钱和账单

英国汽油和柴油价格能涨到多高？

天然气和电力价格发生了什么变化？

还有哪些因素推高通胀？

尽管通胀自 2022 年 10 月高点以来已大幅下降，但价格并未下跌。它们只是增长速度较慢而已。

2022 年通胀飙升，原因是新冠大流行后对石油和天然气的需求增加，以及俄罗斯入侵乌克兰时能源价格再次飙升。

随后其仍远高于 2% 的目标，部分原因是食品价格上涨。

数据显示，食品价格通胀率从截至 4 月份的 3% 降至截至 5 月份的 2.2%。

面包、肉类、面食和蔬菜的通胀率均较去年有所下降。

农业和园艺发展委员会表示，英国家庭目前难以买得起牛肉，这意味着对该产品的需求已经下降。

与此同时，虽然被屠宰的奶牛数量有所下降，但尸体的重量却增加了，因此出现了供应过剩。

英国国家统计局表示，5 月份牛肉和小牛肉（将两者合并在一起）的价格上涨了 9.3%，无可否认，这个价格仍然很高。

但与前几个月价格上涨高达 27% 的情况相比，增速放缓是显而易见的。

然而，食品供应链的成本增加可能需要 7 到 13 个月的时间才能影响超市货架上的价格。

代表制造商的食品和饮料联合会表示，目前的食品价格并未反映霍尔木兹海峡关闭的影响。

它警告称，今年直至 2027 年食品通胀可能会上升。

生活成本较高的员工更有可能推动加薪。

由于雇主增加国民保险缴款和提高最低工资，已经面临员工成本上升的公司将面临提高价格的压力。这反过来又会导致更高的通货膨胀。

为什么加息有助于降低通胀？

英国央行将于周四决定是否提高、降低或维持基本利率。

当通胀远高于2%的目标时，英国央行将利率提高至5.25%，创16年来新高。

这个想法是让借贷变得更加昂贵，让人们和企业花更少的钱。这可以鼓励人们增加储蓄。

反过来，这减少了商品需求并减缓了价格上涨。

但这是一种平衡行为——增加借贷成本可能会损害经济。

例如，房主面临更高的抵押贷款还款额，这可能比更好的储蓄交易更重要。

企业借贷也减少，从而降低了创造就业机会的可能性。有些可能会裁员、减少投资。

近几个月来，通胀率一直高于央行目标。与此同时，经济保持相对平稳，就业市场疲软。

因此，尽管通胀高企，央行仍选择降息，以鼓励经济增长。
whyItMatters: 这条信息属于全球新闻信号，适合用于观察外部环境、政策变化、地缘风险和市场情绪。
